_readme: Use entries[] as full word records. Merge each object into Database.json by id (replace matching element).

entries.exampleSentence: The student thought her grandiloquence would make her sound smart, but neither the class nor the teacher bought it.
entries.etymology: Latin *grandiloquus* ‘grand-speaking,’ from *grandis* ‘great’ + *loqui* ‘speak.’

memoryHook.text: "grand" (great) + "loqu" (speak) = speaking in a massive, overly pompous way.

entries.definition: On a magnificent or exaggerated scale
entries.exampleSentence: Margaret planned a grandiose party, replete with elephants, trapeze artists, and clowns.
entries.etymology: French *grandiose*, from Italian *grandioso*, from Latin *grandis* ‘great.’

entries.exampleSentence: Every morning the guy at the donut shop gives me a gratuitous helping of ketchup packets.
entries.etymology: Old French *gratuit*, from Latin *gratuitus* ‘free of charge,’ from *gratia* ‘favor, thanks.’

memoryHook.text: Gratuity - adding an unnecessary, uncalled-for extra tip when the service was already bad.

entries.definition: Drawn to the company of others, sociable
entries.exampleSentence: Well, if you’re not gregarious, I don’t know why you would want to go to a singles party!
entries.etymology: Latin *gregarius* ‘belonging to a flock,’ from *grex* ‘herd, flock.’

memoryHook.text: "greg" (flock) = loving to be part of the flock; highly sociable and outgoing.

entries.definition: Injurious, hurtful; serious or grave in nature
entries.exampleSentence: Electrocuting the inmate without being sure of his guilt would be a truly grievous mistake.
entries.etymology: Anglo-French *grevous*, from *grever* ‘to burden,’ Latin *gravare* ‘make heavy.’

memoryHook.text: Grief - an injury or mistake so severe that it causes immense grief.

entries.exampleSentence: Because of his great guile, the politician was able to survive scandal after scandal.
entries.etymology: Middle English from Old French *guile*, of Germanic origin akin to *wile.*

memoryHook.text: Disguise - a criminal using a clever disguise out of pure guile and trickery.

entries.exampleSentence: A girl can only hear “I love you” so many times before it begins to sound hackneyed and meaningless.
entries.etymology: From *hackney* ‘ordinary riding horse for hire,’ extended to anything overused.

memoryHook.text: From Hackney, an area in London where horses were bred to be rented out. The horses became so overworked and common that the word evolved…

entries.exampleSentence: In the hallowed corridors of the cathedral, the disturbed professor felt himself to be at peace.
entries.etymology: Old English *halgian* ‘to make holy,’ from *hālig* ‘holy.’

memoryHook.text: Halloween (All Hallows' Eve) - referring to the most holy, deeply respected evening.

entries.exampleSentence: My poor, hapless family never seems to pick a sunny week to go on vacation.
entries.etymology: From *hap* ‘luck’ (Old Norse *happ*) + *-less* ‘without.’

memoryHook.text: "hap" (luck/chance) + "less" = entirely without luck; doomed.

entries.etymology: Middle French *harangue*, from medieval Latin *haringa* ‘troop in line’ (a military address).

senses.exampleSentence: Everyone had heard the teacher’s harangue about gum chewing in class before.
But this time the teacher harangued the class about the importance of brushing your teeth after chewing gum.

memoryHook.text: Hear anger - a long, aggressive speech where all you hear is the speaker's intense anger.

entries.exampleSentence: Everyone had heard the teacher’s harangue about gum chewing in class before.

entries.definition: Robust, capable of surviving through adverse conditions
entries.exampleSentence: I too would have expected the plants to be dead by mid-November, but apparently they’re very hardy.
entries.etymology: Middle English *hardi*, from Old French *hardi* ‘bold,’ from Frankish *hardjan* ‘make hard.’

memoryHook.text: Hard - a plant or person tough enough to survive extremely hard, freezing conditions.

entries.exampleSentence: The car crash was a harrowing experience, but I have a feeling that the increase in my insurance premiums will be even more upsetting.
entries.etymology: From *harrow* ‘to plow up’ (figuratively torment); Old English *hergian* ‘to harry.’

memoryHook.text: Arrowing - being shot at by a rain of flying arrows is a terrifying, highly distressing experience.

entries.exampleSentence: The superstar’s haughty dismissal of her costars will backfire on her someday.
entries.etymology: Extension of *haught* ‘high,’ from Old French *haut* ‘high,’ Latin *altus.*

memoryHook.text: High - acting incredibly high and mighty, looking down on everyone else.

entries.definition: One who believes pleasure should be the primary pursuit.
entries.exampleSentence: Because he’s such a hedonist, I knew Murray would appreciate the 11 cases of wine I bought him for his birthday.
entries.etymology: Greek *hēdonē* ‘pleasure’ + *-ist’; coined for seekers of pleasure as a principle.

memoryHook.text: He done it - "He done it" just for the sheer thrill, because a hedonist lives strictly for physical pleasure.

entries.exampleSentence: Britain’s hegemony over its colonies was threatened once nationalist sentiment began to spread around the world.
entries.etymology: Greek *hēgemonia* ‘leadership,’ from *hēgemōn* ‘leader,’ from *hēgeisthai* ‘to lead.’

memoryHook.text: Huge money - the empire with huge money has absolute dominance and control over the weaker nations.

entries.exampleSentence: The killings were made all the more heinous by the fact that the murderer first tortured his victims for three days.
entries.etymology: Old French *haineus*, from *haine* ‘hatred,’ from Frankish *hatis* ‘hatred.’

memoryHook.text: Hate us - committing crimes so terribly wicked they make the entire world hate us.

entries.exampleSentence: I hate having only one flavor so I always buy the swirled, or should I say heterogeneous, type of ice cream.
entries.etymology: Greek *heterogenēs*, from *heteros* ‘other’ + *genos* ‘kind.’

memoryHook.text: "hetero" (different) + "gen" (kind) = made up of completely different, varied parts.

entries.definition: A break or gap in duration or continuity
entries.exampleSentence: The hiatus in service should last two or three months—until the cable lines are repaired .
entries.etymology: Latin *hiatus* ‘opening, gap,’ from *hiare* ‘to gape.’

memoryHook.text: Hide us - the famous band begging "hide us" so they can take a long, badly needed break from the public.

entries.definition: A system with ranked groups, usually according to.
entries.exampleSentence: Women found it very difficult to break into the upper ranks of the department’s hierarchy.
entries.etymology: Greek *hierarchia* ‘rule of a high priest,’ from *hieros* ‘sacred’ + *archē* ‘rule.’

entries.definition: Pretending to believe what one does not
entries.exampleSentence: Once the politician began passing legislation that contradicted his campaign promises, his hypocrisy became apparent.
entries.etymology: Greek *hypokrisis* ‘acting on a stage,’ from *hypo-* ‘under’ + *krinein* ‘to separate, judge.’

entries.definition: Supposed or assumed true, but unproven
entries.exampleSentence: Even though it has been celebrated by seven major newspapers, that the drug will be a success when tested in humans is still hypothetical.
entries.etymology: Greek *hupothetikos*, from *hupotithenai* ‘to place under, suppose.’

entries.definition: One who attacks common beliefs or institutions
entries.exampleSentence: Jane goes to one protest after another, but she seems to be an iconoclast rather than an activist with a progressive agenda.
entries.etymology: Greek *eikōn* ‘image’ + *klan* ‘to break’—one who smashes idols or conventions.

memoryHook.text: "icon" (image) + "clast" (breaker) = a rebel who attacks and breaks heavily established traditions and beliefs.

entries.definition: Peculiar to one person; highly individualized
entries.exampleSentence: I know you had trouble with the last test, but because your mistakes were highly idiosyncratic, I’m going to deny your request that the class be given a new test.
entries.etymology: Greek *idios* ‘one’s own’ + *synkrasis* ‘mixture’—a personal blend of traits.

memoryHook.text: "idio" (peculiar/self) = a highly unique, strange trait specific to one individual.

entries.definition: Excessively worshipping one object or person
entries.exampleSentence: Xena’s idolatrous fawning over the band—following them on tour, starting their fan club, filming their documentary—is really beginning to get on my nerves.
entries.etymology: Greek *eidōlolatreia* ‘image worship,’ from *eidōlon* ‘image’ + *latreia* ‘service.’

memoryHook.text: "idol" = worshiping an idol or a person to an excessive, blind degree.

entries.exampleSentence: It was really ignominious to be kicked out of the dorm for having an illegal gas stove in my room.
entries.etymology: Latin *ignominiosus*, from *in-* ‘not’ + *nomen* ‘name’—loss of good name.

memoryHook.text: "ig-" (not) + "nomin" (name) = having your good name totally destroyed; suffering a disgraceful public defeat.

entries.exampleSentence: The fourth-grader learned many illicit words from a pamphlet that was being passed around school.
entries.etymology: Latin *illicitus* ‘not allowed,’ from *in-* + *licitus* ‘permitted.’

memoryHook.text: Ill - doing highly illegal things that make society ill.

entries.exampleSentence: After breaking up with her boyfriend, Nancy decided to immerse herself in her work in order to avoid crying.
entries.etymology: Latin *immergere*, from *in-* + *mergere* ‘to dip, plunge.’

entries.exampleSentence: The laws of physics are immutable and constant.
entries.etymology: Latin *immutabilis*, from *in-* + *mutare* ‘to change.’

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "mut" (change) = a law of physics that is entirely unable to be mutated or changed.

entries.exampleSentence: Stop being so impassive; it’s healthy to cry every now and then.
entries.etymology: From *in-* ‘not’ + *passive* (Latin *pati* ‘to suffer, feel’).

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pass" (feeling) = an expression showing absolutely zero emotion or feeling.

entries.exampleSentence: If your grades were as impeccable as your sister’s, then you too would receive a car for a graduation present.
entries.etymology: Latin *impeccabilis*, from *in-* + *peccare* ‘to sin, stumble.’

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pecc" (sin) = a performance or outfit that is entirely without sin or flaw.

entries.exampleSentence: “I fear he’s too impecunious to take me out tonight,” the bratty girl whined.
entries.etymology: From Latin *pecunia* ‘money’ + *in-* ‘without’—without money.

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pecun" (money) = having absolutely no money.

entries.etymology: Late Latin *imperativus* ‘commanding,’ from *imperare* ‘to command.’

senses.exampleSentence: It is imperative that you have these folders organized by midday.
Her imperative to have the folders organized by midday was perceived as ridiculous by the others.

entries.exampleSentence: It is imperative that you have these folders organized by midday.
The imperious nature of your manner led me to dislike you at once.
entries.etymology: Latin *imperiosus* ‘commanding,’ from *imperium* ‘command, empire.’

memoryHook.text: Emperor - acting like a bossy, highly arrogant emperor barking orders.

entries.exampleSentence: Most of your comments are so impertinent that I don’t wish to dignify them with an answer.
entries.etymology: Latin *impertinens* ‘not belonging,’ from *in-* + *pertinere* ‘to pertain’—out of place.

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pertinent" (proper/relevant) = acting outside the bounds of proper respect; incredibly rude to an authority figure.

entries.exampleSentence: Because of their thick layer of fur, many seals are almost impervious to the cold.
entries.etymology: Latin *impervius*, from *in-* + *pervius* ‘passable,’ from *per-* ‘through’ + *via* ‘way.’

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "per" (through) + "via" (way) = providing no way through; a raincoat impervious to water.

entries.exampleSentence: Hilda’s hasty slaying of the king was an impetuous, thoughtless action.
entries.etymology: Late Latin *impetuosus*, from Latin *impetus* ‘attack, rush.’

memoryHook.text: Impulse - acting strictly on wild impulse without thinking of the consequences.

entries.etymology: Latin *impingere* ‘to drive in, strike against,’ from *in-* + *pangere* ‘to fasten, fix.’

senses.definition: To impact, affect, make an impression
senses.exampleSentence: The hail impinged the roof, leaving large dents.
I apologize for impinging upon you like this, but I really need to use your bathroom. Now.

memoryHook.text: Pinch - encroaching on someone's personal space until you literally pinch them.

entries.definition: To impact, affect, make an impression
entries.exampleSentence: The hail impinged the roof, leaving large dents.

entries.definition: Incapable of being appeased or mitigated
entries.exampleSentence: Watch out: once you shun Grandma’s cooking, she is totally implacable.
entries.etymology: Latin *implacabilis*, from *in-* + *placare* ‘to appease.’

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "plac" (calm/please) = an enemy whose raging anger is impossible to calm down.

entries.etymology: Late Latin *implēmentum* ‘filling up,’ from *implēre* ‘to fulfill’ (*in-* + *plēre* ‘fill’).

senses.exampleSentence: Do you have a knife or some other sort of implement that I could use to pry the lid off of this jar?

senses.definition: To put into effect, to institute
senses.exampleSentence: After the first town curfew failed to stop the graffiti problem, the mayor implemented a new policy to use security cameras to catch perpetrators in the act.

entries.exampleSentence: Do you have a knife or some other sort of implement that I could use to pry the lid off of this jar?

entries.definition: To involve in an incriminating way, incriminate
entries.exampleSentence: Even though Tom wasn’t present at the time of the shooting, he was implicated by the evidence suggesting that he had supplied the shooters with guns.
entries.etymology: Latin *implicare* ‘to fold in,’ from *in-* + *plicare* ‘to fold.’

entries.definition: Understood but not outwardly obvious, implied
entries.exampleSentence: I know Professor Smith didn’t actually say not to write from personal experience, but I think such a message was implicit in her instruction to use scholarly sources.
entries.etymology: Latin *implicitus* ‘folded in,’ past participle of *implicare*.

entries.exampleSentence: Though the invaders used battering rams, catapults, and rain dances, the fortress proved impregnable and resisted all attacks.
entries.etymology: Old French *impregnable*, from *in-* + *pregnable* ‘able to be taken’ (from *prendre* ‘take’).

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pregnable" (vulnerable to capture) = a heavy stone fortress that is impossible to break into.

entries.exampleSentence: The impudent young man looked the princess up and down and told her she was hot even though she hadn’t asked him.
entries.etymology: Latin *impudens* ‘shameless,’ from *in-* + *pudens* ‘modest,’ from *pudere* ‘to be ashamed.’

memoryHook.text: Rude student - a highly bold, shamelessly disrespectful student backtalking the principal.

entries.exampleSentence: The CEO imputed the many typos in the letter to his lazy secretary.
entries.etymology: Latin *imputare* ‘to reckon, charge,’ from *in-* + *putare* ‘to think, reckon.’

memoryHook.text: Put - to put the blame for the mistake directly onto someone else's record.

entries.exampleSentence: Some films are so inane that the psychology of the characters makes absolutely no sense.
entries.etymology: Latin *inanis* ‘empty, void.’

memoryHook.text: Insane - a comment so painfully empty and silly it borders on insane.

entries.definition: Incapable of expressing oneself clearly through speech
entries.exampleSentence: Though he spoke for over an hour, the lecturer was completely inarticulate and the students had no idea what he was talking about.
entries.etymology: Latin *inarticulatus* ‘not jointed,’ from *in-* + *articulare* ‘to speak distinctly.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "articulate" = entirely unable to speak clearly or express thoughts.

entries.etymology: Latin *incarnare* ‘to make flesh,’ from *in-* + *caro* ‘flesh.’

senses.exampleSentence: In the church pageant, I play the role of greed incarnate.
The alien evaded detection by incarnating himself in a human form.

memoryHook.text: "in-" (in) + "carn" (flesh) = a spirit or idea given physical, bodily form in the flesh.

entries.exampleSentence: In the church pageant, I play the role of greed incarnate.

entries.etymology: Latin *incendiarius*, from *incendium* ‘conflagration,’ from *incendere* ‘to set on fire.’

senses.exampleSentence: If we catch the incendiary who screamed “bomb” in the middle of the soccer match, we’re going to put him in jail.
Gas and lighter fluid are incendiary materials that should be kept out of hot storage areas.

memoryHook.text: "incend" (fire) = a bomb or a speech specifically designed to cause massive fires or conflict.

entries.exampleSentence: If we catch the incendiary who screamed “bomb” in the middle of the soccer match, we’re going to put him in jail.
We wanted to go outside and play, but the incessantrain kept us indoors for two days.
entries.etymology: Late Latin *incessans*, from *in-* + *cessare* ‘to cease.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "cess" (stop) = a dripping faucet that is never stopping.

entries.definition: Unformed or formless, in a beginning stage
entries.exampleSentence: The country’s government is still inchoate and, because it has no great tradition, quite unstable.
entries.etymology: Latin *inchoatus* ‘begun,’ from *inchoare* ‘to start work’ (origin disputed).

memoryHook.text: Inches - a business plan that is only a few inches along; just barely starting and completely unformed.

entries.exampleSentence: The discussion wasn’t going anywhere until her incisive comment allowed everyone to see what the true issues were.
entries.etymology: Latin *incisus* ‘cut into,’ from *incidere*, from *in-* + *caedere* ‘to cut.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (into) + "caedere" (cut) = mental analysis that cuts straight to the heart of the matter; razor-sharp.

entries.exampleSentence: Sarah has an inclination to see every foreign film she hears about, even when she’s sure that she won’t like it.
entries.etymology: Latin *inclinatio*, from *inclinare* ‘to lean toward.’

entries.exampleSentence: Only stubborn Tina would attempt to disprove the incontrovertible laws of physics.
entries.etymology: Latin *incontrovertibilis*, from *in-* + *controvertere* ‘to dispute.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "controvert" (dispute) = DNA evidence that is entirely impossible to argue against.

entries.exampleSentence: You can buy Grandma nicotine gum all you want, but I think that after sixty-five years of smoking she’s incorrigible.
entries.etymology: Latin *incorrigibilis*, from *in-* + *corrigere* ‘to correct.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "corrigere" (correct) = a criminal so deeply bad they are completely beyond correction.

entries.definition: An enlargement; the process of increasing
entries.exampleSentence: The workmen made the wall longer, increment by increment.
entries.etymology: Latin *incrementum* ‘growth,’ from *increscere* ‘to grow upon.’
Latin *incumbere* ‘to lie upon,’ hence pressing as a duty.

senses.exampleSentence: The incumbent senator is already serving his fifth term.
It is incumbent upon this organization to offer aid to all who seek it.

memoryHook.text: Occupies the seat now - the officeholder already in power (the one whose role is filled).

entries.exampleSentence: The incumbent senator is already serving his fifth term.
Even after traveling 62 miles, the indefatigable runner kept on moving.
entries.etymology: Latin *indefatigabilis*, from *in-* + *defatigare* ‘to wear out.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "defatigate" (tire) = a marathon runner whose energy is impossible to tire out.

entries.exampleSentence: Some fear that these plants, which are not indigenous to the region, may choke out the vegetation that is native to the area.
entries.etymology: Late Latin *indigena* ‘native,’ from *indi-* ‘within’ + *gen-* ‘be born.’

entries.exampleSentence: I would rather donate money to help the indigent population than to the park sculpture fund.
entries.etymology: Old French *indigent*, from Latin *indigere* ‘to need,’ literally ‘to lack in.’

memoryHook.text: Dig in dirt - someone so desperately poor they have to dig in the dirt to find food.

entries.definition: Anger sparked by something unjust or unfair
entries.exampleSentence: I resigned from the sorority because of my indignation at its hazing of new members.
entries.etymology: Latin *indignatio*, from *indignari* ‘to regard as unworthy,’ from *in-* + *dignus* ‘worthy.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "dign" (worthy) = fierce anger sparked by being treated in a completely unworthy or unfair way.

entries.exampleSentence: Why should my indolent children, who can’t even pick themselves up off the couch to pour their own juice, be rewarded with a trip to the mall?
entries.etymology: Late Latin *indolens* ‘free from pain,’ from *in-* + *dolere* ‘to suffer’—avoiding effort.

memoryHook.text: Indoor tent - laying lazily in an indoor tent all day doing absolutely nothing.

entries.exampleSentence: To be honest, Jim, my indomitable nature means I could never take orders from anyone, and especially not from a jerk like you.
entries.etymology: Late Latin *indomitabilis*, from *in-* + *domitare* ‘to tame,’ frequentative of *domare*.

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "domit" (tame) = an underdog spirit that is impossible to crush or tame.

entries.exampleSentence: Who knew that our decision to boycott school lunch would induce a huge riot?
entries.etymology: Latin *inducere* ‘to lead in,’ from *in-* + *ducere* ‘to lead.’

entries.definition: Unspeakable, incapable of being expressed through words
entries.exampleSentence: It is said that the experience of playing with a dolphin is ineffable and can only be understood through direct encounter.
entries.etymology: Latin *ineffabilis*, from *in-* + *effari* ‘to speak out.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "effable" (speakable) = looking at the Grand Canyon and feeling an awe so immense it cannot be expressed in words.

entries.exampleSentence: She proved how inept she was when she forgot three orders and spilled a beer in a customer’s lap.
entries.etymology: Latin *ineptus* ‘unsuited,’ from *in-* + *aptus* ‘fitted.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "apt" (skilled) = entirely clumsy and lacking any skill for the job.

entries.definition: Incapable of being persuaded or placated
entries.exampleSentence: Although I begged for hours, Mom was inexorable and refused to let me stay out all night after the prom.
entries.etymology: Latin *inexorabilis*, from *in-* + *exorare* ‘to obtain by entreaty’—not moved by pleading.

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "exorare" (persuade) = the marching army was impossible to persuade or stop.

entries.exampleSentence: Unless I look at the solution manual, I have no way of solving this inextricable problem.
entries.etymology: Latin *inextricabilis*, from *in-* + *extricare* ‘to disentangle.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "extricate" (untangle) = two headphones hopelessly and impossibly tangled together.

entries.exampleSentence: The infamy of his crime will not lessen as the decades pass.
entries.etymology: Latin *infamia*, from *in-* + *fama* ‘report, reputation.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "fame" = possessing world fame, but strictly for highly evil reasons.

entries.definition: An injection of one substance into another; the.
entries.exampleSentence: The infusion of Eastern religion into Western philosophy created interesting new schools of thought.
entries.etymology: Latin *infusio*, from *infundere* ‘to pour in,’ from *in-* + *fundere* ‘pour.’

entries.exampleSentence: Her ingenious use of walnuts instead of the peanuts called for by the recipe was lauded by the other garden club members who found her cake delicious.
entries.etymology: Latin *ingeniosus* ‘talented,’ from *ingenium* ‘innate quality, wit.’

entries.exampleSentence: He must have writers, but his speeches seem so ingenuous it’s hard to believe he’s not speaking from his own heart.
entries.etymology: Latin *ingenuus* ‘native, freeborn,’ hence frank and open.

memoryHook.text: Genuine - someone with a highly innocent, trusting, and completely genuine nature.

entries.exampleSentence: When I told you I needed the car last night, I certainly never meant to inhibit you from going out.
entries.etymology: Latin *inhibere* ‘to hold in,’ from *in-* + *habere* ‘to hold.’

entries.exampleSentence: I don’t see how I could ever work for a company that was so cold and inimical to me during my interviews.
entries.etymology: Late Latin *inimicalis*, from Latin *inimicus* ‘enemy,’ from *in-* + *amicus* ‘friend.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "amicus" (friend) = deeply unfriendly, hostile, and actively harmful.

entries.exampleSentence: “Your iniquity,” said the priest to the practical jokester, “will be forgiven.”
entries.etymology: Old French *iniquité*, from Latin *iniquitas* ‘unfairness,’ from *iniquus* ‘unequal, unjust.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "equ" (fair/equal) = a situation of gross injustice or deeply wicked behavior.

entries.exampleSentence: After his house was toilet-papered for the fifth time, the mayor issued an injunction against anyone younger than 21 buying toilet paper.
entries.etymology: Late Latin *iniunctio*, from Latin *iniungere* ‘to enjoin,’ from *in-* + *iungere* ‘join.’

entries.exampleSentence: His incredible athletic talent is innate, he never trains, lifts weights, or practices.
entries.etymology: Latin *innatus* ‘inborn,’ past participle of *innasci* ‘to be born in.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (in) + "nat" (born) = a perfect pitch musical talent you were naturally born with.

entries.exampleSentence: In spite of their innocuous appearance, these mushrooms are actually quite poisonous.
entries.etymology: Latin *innocuus* ‘harmless,’ from *in-* + *nocuus* ‘hurtful,’ from *nocēre* ‘to harm.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "noc" (harm) = entirely safe and harmless.

entries.definition: To do something in an unprecedented way
entries.exampleSentence: Because of the stiff competition, the company knew it needed to pour a lot of energy into innovating new and better products.
entries.etymology: Latin *innovare* ‘to renew,’ from *in-* + *novare* ‘to make new,’ from *novus* ‘new.’

entries.exampleSentence: During the debate, the politician made several innuendos about the sexual activities of his opponent.
entries.etymology: Latin *innuendo* ‘by nodding to,’ ablative gerund of *innuere* ‘to nod to, hint.’

entries.definition: To introduce a microorganism, serum, or vaccine into an.
entries.exampleSentence: I’ve feared needles ever since I was inoculated against 37 diseases at age one; but I have also never been sick.
entries.etymology: Latin *inoculare* ‘to graft an eye (bud),’ from *in-* + *oculus* ‘eye’ (bud of a plant).

entries.definition: One who inquires, especially in a hostile manner
entries.exampleSentence: The inquisitor was instructed to knock on every door in town in order to find the fugitive.
entries.etymology: Latin *inquisitor*, from *inquirere* ‘to seek into,’ from *in-* + *quaerere* ‘ask.’

entries.exampleSentence: My insatiable appetite for melons can be a real problem in the winter.
entries.etymology: Latin *insatiabilis*, from *in-* + *satiare* ‘to fill.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "satiable" (satisfied) = a hunger for power that is absolutely impossible to satisfy.

entries.exampleSentence: Lisa’s insidious chocolate cake tastes so good but makes you feel so sick later on!
entries.etymology: Latin *insidiosus* ‘cunning,’ from *insidiae* ‘ambush,’ from *insidere* ‘to sit in.’

memoryHook.text: Inside us - a dangerous, sneaky disease spreading quietly inside us without being noticed until it's too late.

entries.exampleSentence: I wish Luke and Spencer would stop insinuating that my perfect report card is the result of anything other than my superior intelligence and good work habits.
entries.etymology: Latin *insinuare* ‘to wind in,’ from *in-* + *sinus* ‘curve, fold.’

entries.exampleSentence: The play was so insipid, I fell asleep halfway through.
entries.etymology: French *insipide*, from Late Latin *insipidus*, from *in-* + *sapidus* ‘tasty,’ from *sapere* ‘to taste.’

memoryHook.text: "ob" (against) + "fuscare" (darken) = to completely darken or hide the truth to confuse the public.

entries.definition: Diverging from a straight line or course, not.
entries.exampleSentence: Martin’s oblique language confused those who listened to him.
entries.etymology: Latin *obliquus* slides at angles rather than confronting head-on.

memoryHook.text: Ob-look - taking an indirect, highly slanted look at the problem rather than attacking it straight on.

entries.definition: Lacking consciousness or awareness of something
entries.exampleSentence: Oblivious to the burning smell emanating from the kitchen, my father did not notice that the rolls in the oven were burned until much too late.
entries.etymology: *Oblīvīscī* swallows surroundings until nothing peripheral registers.

entries.exampleSentence: Because he was standing in the shadows, his features were obscure.
entries.etymology: *Obscurus* piles shadow until interpreters must grope deliberately.

entries.exampleSentence: Mark acted like Janet’s servant, obeying her every request in an obsequious manner.
entries.etymology: *Ob-* plus *sequī* describes followers tripping over themselves to please power.

memoryHook.text: Obsessed yes - fawning on authority, agreeing endlessly to stay in the boss's good graces.

entries.definition: No longer used, out of date
entries.exampleSentence: With the inventions of tape decks and CDs, which both have better sound and are easier to use, eight-track players are now entirely obsolete.
entries.etymology: *Obsolesce* marks tools or words shelved dusty past usefulness.

entries.exampleSentence: The obstinate child refused to leave the store until his mother bought him a candy bar.
entries.etymology: *Obstināre* plants feet like stakes against persuasion’s wind.

entries.exampleSentence: Billy’s obstreperous behavior prompted the librarian to ask him to leave the reading room.
entries.etymology: Latin noise roots named crowds that shout down order itself.

memoryHook.text: Street prep - a loud, completely unruly crowd causing wild chaos while prepping in the street.

entries.definition: Lacking quickness of sensibility or intellect
entries.exampleSentence: Political opponents warned that the prime minister’s obtuse approach to foreign policy would embroil the nation in mindless war.
entries.etymology: *Obtūsus* blunted edges—whether angles or minds missing nuance.

entries.exampleSentence: Mark was assigned the odious task of cleaning the cat’s litter box.
entries.etymology: *Odisse* names hatred plain on the tongue.

memoryHook.text: Odor - a personality or crime so deeply repulsive it hits you like a foul, sickening odor.

entries.definition: Offering one’s services when they are neither wanted nor.

entries.etymology: Latin *officium* twisted into busybody zeal ‘helping’ without welcome.

memoryHook.text: Office boss - an annoying, low-level coworker acting exactly like the office boss and aggressively meddling in your work.

entries.exampleSentence: The fortuneteller’s ominous words flashed through my mind as the hooded figure approached me in the alley.
entries.etymology: *Omen* shadows events that feel heavy with coming harm.

memoryHook.text: Omen - dark storm clouds giving a threatening omen that something very bad is coming.

entries.exampleSentence: My parents lamented that the pleasures of living in a beautiful country estate no longer outweighed the onerous mortgage payments.
entries.etymology: Latin *onus* loads backs until every step costs effort.

memoryHook.text: On us - an incredibly heavy, punishingly difficult burden placed squarely on us.

entries.definition: Characterized by rich abundance verging on ostentation
entries.exampleSentence: The opulent furnishings of the dictator’s private compound contrasted harshly with the meager accommodations of her subjects.
entries.etymology: Latin *ops* (‘wealth’) spills outward in visible excess.

memoryHook.text: Opal - a massive mansion dripping in opals, diamonds, and sheer, excessive wealth.

entries.definition: A speech delivered in a formal or ceremonious manner

entries.etymology: Latin *ōrāre* supplied formal speech measured for public ears.

entries.exampleSentence: The ornate styling of the new model of luxury car could not compensate for the poor quality of its motor.
entries.etymology: Ornament stacked on ornament until surfaces glitter with filigree.

entries.exampleSentence: The company’s profits dwindled because the management pursued orthodox business policies that were incompatible with new industrial trends.
entries.etymology: Greek *orthos* plus *doxa* aligned belief with received teaching.

memoryHook.text: "ortho" (straight/correct) + "dox" (belief) = following the strictly established, completely traditional beliefs.

entries.definition: To sway from one side to the other
entries.exampleSentence: My uncle oscillated between buying a station wagon to transport his family and buying a sports car to satisfy his boyhood fantasies.
entries.etymology: Latin *oscillum* was a little swinging mask—motion that never locks still.

entries.exampleSentence: Jack’s ostensible reason for driving was that airfare was too expensive, but in reality, he was afraid of flying.
entries.etymology: What shows on the surface may cloak motives still tucked below deck.

memoryHook.text: Sensible - presenting a sensible-sounding, fake excuse that completely hides your true motive.

entries.exampleSentence: On the palace tour, the guide focused on the ostentatious decorations and spoke little of the royal family’s history.
entries.etymology: Display curdles into performance meant to dazzle wallets or rivals.

memoryHook.text: Ostrich - walking around in an enormous ostrich-feather hat specifically to aggressively show off your wealth.

entries.exampleSentence: Beth risked ostracism if her roommates discovered her flatulence.
entries.etymology: Athenian potsherd votes could banish citizens—metaphor still expels pariahs.

memoryHook.text: From ancient Athens, where citizens voted to banish dangerous politicians from the city by writing their names on pieces of broken pottery…

entries.exampleSentence: The chemistry professor’s pacific demeanor helped the class remain calm after the experiment exploded.
entries.etymology: Latin *pāx* steers temper toward dove-gray calm instead of war heat.

memoryHook.text: "pac" (peace) = a demeanor that is deeply calm, tranquil, and peaceful (like the Pacific Ocean).

entries.definition: Agreeable to the taste or sensibilities
entries.exampleSentence: Despite the unpleasant smell, the exotic cheese was quite palatable.
entries.etymology: *Palātum* tests whether ideas or flavors please the tongue—or mind.

memoryHook.text: Plate - the strange food on the plate is finally agreeable and okay to your taste.

entries.definition: A range of colors or qualities

entries.exampleSentence: The doctor trusted that the new medication would palliate her patient’s discomfort.
entries.etymology: Latin *pallium* cloaked severity—symptoms eased even when causes linger.

memoryHook.text: Pill ate - you ate a pill to soothe and hide the severe symptoms, even though it didn't actually cure the disease.

entries.exampleSentence: Dr. Van Helsing feared that Lucy’s pallid complexion was due to an unexplained loss of blood.
entries.etymology: Latin *pallidus* drained color as if fear bleached the cheeks.

memoryHook.text: Pale - a completely pale, sickly, and utterly lifeless face.

entries.definition: A remedy for all ills or difficulties

entries.definition: An example that is a perfect pattern or model

entries.etymology: Greek *parádeigma* exhibited patterns others copy deliberately.

entries.definition: An apparently contradictory statement that is perhaps true
entries.exampleSentence: The diplomat refused to acknowledge the paradox that negotiating a peace treaty would demand more resources than waging war.
entries.etymology: Greek roots describe opinions bucking plausible expectation—not mere puzzles.

entries.definition: A model of excellence or perfection
entries.exampleSentence: The mythical Helen of Troy was considered a paragon of female beauty.
entries.etymology: Old French *paragon* measured gems on touchstones until one surpassed every rival held beside it.

entries.exampleSentence: It was paramount that the bomb squad disconnect the blue wire before removing the fuse.
entries.etymology: Anglo-Norman *paramont* perched authority at the height above subordinates.

memoryHook.text: Mountain - sitting at the absolute highest, most supreme mountain peak of importance.

entries.exampleSentence: Following the discovery of his plagiarism, Professor Hurley was made a pariah in all academic circles.

entries.exampleSentence: A hush fell over the classroom when the teacher returned to find Deborah acting out a parody of his teaching style.
entries.etymology: Greek *parā-* plus *ōidḗ* twisted songs into mocking echoes of originals.

entries.exampleSentence: Many relatives believed that my aunt’s wealth resulted from her parsimony.
entries.etymology: Latin *parsus* pinched every coin until generosity starved.

memoryHook.text: Purse money - tightly and fiercely guarding your purse money because you are incredibly cheap.

entries.exampleSentence: The king did not believe that his rival could round up enough partisans to overthrow the monarchy.

entries.exampleSentence: The reason for Jim’s abdominal pain was made patent after the doctor performed a sonogram.
entries.etymology: Latin *patēre* means ‘lie open,’ so truth lies exposed beyond dispute.

entries.exampleSentence: Dr. Hastings had difficulty identifying the precise nature of Brian’s pathology.
entries.etymology: Greek *pathos* plus *logos* names the disciplined study of what injuries bodies or institutions.

entries.exampleSentence: Martha filled with pathos upon discovering the scrawny, shivering kitten at her door.
entries.etymology: *Páthos* names suffering leveraged onstage—or in rhetoric—to wring humane response.

entries.exampleSentence: Gilbert lamented the paucity of twentieth century literature courses available at the college.

entries.exampleSentence: The evening’s headline news covered an international scandal caused by a pejorative statement the famous senator had made in reference to a foreign leader.

memoryHook.text: Major dive - an insulting, highly negative word that makes your public reputation take a major dive.

entries.exampleSentence: Wishing his book to be pellucid to the common man, Albert Camus avoided using complicated grammar when composing The Stranger.
entries.etymology: *Per-* intensifies *lūcēre*: prose so lucent readers never grope blindly.

memoryHook.text: "per" (through) + "luc" (light) = water or writing letting maximum light through; perfectly clear and easy to understand.

entries.exampleSentence: Jill’s dinner parties quickly became monotonous on account of her penchant for Mexican dishes.
entries.etymology: French roots picture hanging weight tipped toward habitual liking.

memoryHook.text: Pendant - wearing a specific, unique pendant every day because you have a massive, natural liking for it.

entries.exampleSentence: The jury’s verdict may have been more lenient if the criminal had appeared penitent for his gruesome crimes.
entries.etymology: Latin *paenitēre* weighs guilt sincerely enough to spur amendment.

memoryHook.text: Penalty tent - sitting alone in the penalty tent feeling incredibly sorry and guilty for your sins.

entries.exampleSentence: Having smoked the penultimate cigarette remaining in the pack, Cybil discarded the last cigarette and resolved to quit smoking.
entries.etymology: *Paene* (‘almost’) with *ultimus* docks one step shy of finale.

entries.exampleSentence: Stella complained that her husband’s penurious ways made it impossible to live the lifestyle she felt she deserved.
entries.etymology: *Pēnia* tethered scarcity of coin—bodily want or stingy instincts alike.

memoryHook.text: Penny furious - a guy who becomes furious if he loses even a single penny because he is desperately, severely poor.

entries.exampleSentence: After the official was caught selling government secrets to enemy agents, he was executed for his perfidious ways.
entries.etymology: Latin *perfidia* violates sworn faith with knife-twist betrayal.

entries.exampleSentence: Mrs. Huxtable was annoyed that the wet dog’s odor had permeated the furniture’s upholstery.

entries.exampleSentence: The new government feared that the Communist sympathizers would have a pernicious influence on the nation’s stability.
entries.etymology: *Per-* plus *necāre* names ruin that wastes strength quietly.

memoryHook.text: "per" (completely) + "nec" (death) = spreading a highly dangerous, quiet, and deadly harm.

entries.exampleSentence: Brad was perplexed by his girlfriend’s suddenly distant manner.
entries.etymology: Latin roots tangle strands until orderly sense trips over itself.

entries.exampleSentence: The detective was too humble to acknowledge that his perspicacity was the reason for his professional success.
entries.etymology: Latin *perspicācitas* builds on *perspicēre* (‘see through’) for minds that sift signal from camouflage.

memoryHook.text: "per" (through) + "spec" (look) = looking straight through the noise with incredibly razor-sharp mental insight.

entries.exampleSentence: My parents forgave Sandra’s pert humor at the dinner table because it had been so long since they had last seen her.
entries.etymology: Older *pert* leaned on Latin *īmpetus* (‘push’) for sass that elbows past etiquette’s restraint.

entries.exampleSentence: Harry’s parents were frustrated with his pertinacious insistence that a monster lived in his closet. Then they opened the closet door and were eaten.
entries.etymology: *Pertināx* keeps its grip even when coaxing waits for exhaustion to loosen it first.

memoryHook.text: Persistent - fiercely and stubbornly holding onto an opinion with massive persistence.

entries.exampleSentence: The actor agreed to accept the role after a two-month perusal of the movie script.
entries.etymology: Anglo-French habits of careful paging treat close reading itself as disciplined judgment work.

entries.definition: Having the tendency to spread throughout
entries.exampleSentence: Stepping off the plane in Havana, I recognized the pervasive odor of sugar cane fields on fire.
entries.etymology: *Pervādere* marched influence through porous corners of life until little stayed untouched.

memoryHook.text: "per" (through) + "vadere" (go) = an idea spreading rapidly and going into absolutely every corner of society.

entries.exampleSentence: The Nanny resigned after she could no longer tolerate the child’s petulance.
entries.etymology: *Petulus* tagged forward moods that snag on petty denials like burrs.

memoryHook.text: Pet - acting exactly like a moody, highly childish pet throwing a wild tantrum.

entries.exampleSentence: Many people felt that the billionaire’s decision to donate her fortune to house the homeless was the ultimate philanthropic act.
entries.etymology: Greek *phílos* plus *ánthrōpos* widened fondness into charity done visibly for strangers’ welfare.

memoryHook.text: "phil" (love) + "anthrop" (human) = giving massive wealth away entirely because of a deep love for humanity.

entries.exampleSentence: Monique feared her dog was ill after the animal’s phlegmatic response to his favorite chew toy.
entries.etymology: Greek *phlégma* named sluggish humors old physiology linked to unruffled—even sleepy—steady moods.

memoryHook.text: From the ancient medical theory of the four humors. Doctors believed having too much "phlegm" in your body meant you had a completely…

entries.definition: To seize or plunder, especially in war
entries.exampleSentence: Invading enemy soldiers pillaged the homes scattered along the country’s border.
entries.etymology: Raids stripping conquered settlements braided violence with systematic hauling away of movable wealth.

entries.exampleSentence: Book reviewers declared that the author’s new novel was extraordinary and probably the pinnacle of Western literature.
entries.etymology: Latin *pīna*, a spike or pin, crowned literal peaks and metaphorical crests alike.

entries.exampleSentence: My father’s long-winded explanation was a stark contrast to his usually pithy statements.
entries.etymology: Old English *pitha* named marrow gnawed from bone—speech pared marrow-clean of needless fat.

memoryHook.text: Pity - it's a pity his speech was so incredibly short, but it was highly dense and effective.

entries.definition: A very small amount, especially relating to money

memoryHook.text: Pity cents - a wage so insultingly tiny they just throw you a few extra cents out of sheer pity.

entries.definition: To ease the anger of, soothe
entries.exampleSentence: The man purchased a lollipop to placate his irritable son.
entries.etymology: Latin *placāre* smooths beasts, crowds, or gods toward soothed acceptance.

memoryHook.text: Play date - setting up a highly fun play date to completely calm down a raging, angry toddler.

entries.exampleSentence: The placid lake surface was as smooth as glass.
entries.etymology: *Placidus* pictured mirror-steady water echoed by moods equally unstirred.

memoryHook.text: Plastic - an ocean surface as completely smooth, calm, and unchanging as solid plastic.

entries.exampleSentence: After reading over her paper, Helene concluded that what she thought were profound insights were actually just platitudes.
entries.etymology: *Plātus* suggests flat stamping—ideas interchangeable as bland metal blanks lacking contour.

memoryHook.text: Flat attitude - offering a flat, highly overused cliché instead of real, helpful advice.

entries.exampleSentence: The controversial new film received plaudits from even the harshest critics.

memoryHook.text: "plaudere" (clap) = receiving heavy, wildly enthusiastic applause and praise from the crowd.

entries.exampleSentence: He studied all the data and then came up with a plausible theory that took all factors into account.
entries.etymology: Latin applause cues hint what earns brief assent—even when sharper audit waits backstage.

entries.exampleSentence: My grandmother was overwhelmed by the plenitude of tomatoes her garden yielded this season.
entries.etymology: *Plētūdō* measures brimful fullness where scarcity cannot find footing.

memoryHook.text: "plen" (full) = a completely full, massive abundance of food.
